<findings>

[observation] [info] Grouping all PRD-authoring input surfaces into one zone makes it straightforward to audit or replace the input layer independently of the read-only PRD tree view.
[observation] [info] Having both batch-import-panel and smart-add-input covered by unit tests while analyze-panel and proposal-editor are not suggests uneven test coverage across the panel set.
[observation] [warning] Mutual imports with web-dashboard-core suggest the panels reach into viewer state rather than receiving it as props/events; a unidirectional data flow pattern would improve testability and zone independence.
[pattern] [warning] analysis.ts (page/view) co-located with panel components inside the same zone blends routing and component responsibilities; extracting the view into web-dashboard-core or a dedicated views zone would likely eliminate one direction of the bidirectional coupling with the core.
[anti-pattern] [warning] analysis.ts is co-located in prd-input-panels but imports BrandedHeader from web-dashboard-core (logos.ts), creating the prd-input-panels → web-dashboard-core coupling direction. BrandedHeader is a pure presentational component with no domain logic; it should be extracted into a shared UI primitives location accessible to all viewer zones without creating cross-zone coupling.
[suggestion] [warning] Add unit tests for analyze-panel.ts and proposal-editor.ts to match the coverage level already established for batch-import-panel.ts and smart-add-input.ts; uneven intra-zone coverage creates a false confidence signal when reviewing the zone's test health.
[suggestion] [info] Rename the zone from 'prd-input-panels' to 'prd-authoring' to accurately reflect its mixed panel+view membership and to avoid implying all members are input widgets.

</findings>

<insights>

- The four panel components share a single view (analysis.ts) as their integration point, which is a clean composition pattern — each panel is independently testable but rendered through one view.
- Bidirectional imports with web-dashboard-core (panel → web-viewer: 2, web-viewer → panel: 2) indicate these panels are tightly coupled to core viewer state; a unidirectional event/callback interface would reduce coupling.
- Cohesion of 0.78 is solid and reflects genuine domain coherence — all components serve the PRD editing and proposal workflow.
- Mutual imports with web-dashboard-core suggest the panels reach into viewer state rather than receiving it as props/events; a unidirectional data flow pattern would improve testability and zone independence.
- Having both batch-import-panel and smart-add-input covered by unit tests while analyze-panel and proposal-editor are not suggests uneven test coverage across the panel set.
- Grouping all PRD-authoring input surfaces into one zone makes it straightforward to audit or replace the input layer independently of the read-only PRD tree view.
- The zone contains analysis.ts classified as a page/view alongside four panel components — mixing page-routing concerns with component concerns inside one zone. The view is the most likely source of the bidirectional coupling with web-dashboard-core, since views typically orchestrate across zone boundaries rather than staying within them.
- analysis.ts (page/view) co-located with panel components inside the same zone blends routing and component responsibilities; extracting the view into web-dashboard-core or a dedicated views zone would likely eliminate one direction of the bidirectional coupling with the core.
- analysis.ts has exactly one cross-zone import: BrandedHeader from components/logos.ts (in web-dashboard-core). This single import is the sole source of the prd-input-panels → web-dashboard-core coupling direction. The fix is bounded: either move logos.ts to a shared assets zone, extract BrandedHeader into a standalone component inside prd-input-panels, or accept the import and merge analysis.ts into web-dashboard-core. No broader refactor is needed.
- analysis.ts contains no routing, navigation state, or orchestration logic — its classification as a 'page' by sourcevision reflects its role as a view-level composition component, not a router concern. The coupling with web-dashboard-core originates from a logo component import, not from page-level state management.
- analysis.ts is co-located in prd-input-panels but imports BrandedHeader from web-dashboard-core (logos.ts), creating the prd-input-panels → web-dashboard-core coupling direction. BrandedHeader is a pure presentational component with no domain logic; it should be extracted into a shared UI primitives location accessible to all viewer zones without creating cross-zone coupling.
- The zone name 'prd-input-panels' is structurally inaccurate: it contains analysis.ts, which is a view-level composition component, not an input panel. The name implies all members are panel widgets, misleading developers scanning zone membership.
- analyze-panel.ts and proposal-editor.ts have no unit tests while batch-import-panel.ts and smart-add-input.ts each have a paired test file — exactly half the panel components are untested, an uneven coverage pattern within a zone that presents all four components as equivalent.
- proposal-editor.ts edits existing analyze output (post-generation editing), while the other three components (batch-import-panel, smart-add-input, analyze-panel) generate new PRD content — the editor operates on a different data lifecycle stage than its zone peers, which may explain why it lacks a test: it has lower conceptual affinity with the other three components than the zone grouping implies.
- Rename the zone from 'prd-input-panels' to 'prd-authoring' to accurately reflect its mixed panel+view membership and to avoid implying all members are input widgets.
- Add unit tests for analyze-panel.ts and proposal-editor.ts to match the coverage level already established for batch-import-panel.ts and smart-add-input.ts; uneven intra-zone coverage creates a false confidence signal when reviewing the zone's test health.
- [call graph] 160 internal calls, 3 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 0.98, coupling: 0.02)

</insights>
